The Evolution of JVM Development

slides.title: 1. Introduction & History
slides.subtitle: From a toolmaker's project to the preferred language for Android

list.text: Interoperable: Use any existing Java library and call Kotlin from Java seamlessly.
Safe: Null safety is built into the type system, preventing the 'billion-dollar mistake'.
Concise: Drastically reduces boilerplate code compared to Java (up to 40% reduction).

box.title: Kotlin is a modern, cross-platform, statically typed programming language developed by **JetBrains**. It is designed to be fully interoperable with Java while providing a more concise, safer, and more expressive developer experience. Since Google's endorsement in 2017, it has become the gold standard for Android development and a powerful choice for server-side and multiplatform projects.

list.text: 2019: Google announces Kotlin as the preferred language for Android.

box.title: Unlike many languages born in research labs or large corporations to solve specific product needs, Kotlin was created by **JetBrains** (the creators of IntelliJ IDEA) to solve their own internal developer productivity issues. They needed a language that was as fast as Java but more modern and easier to tool.

list.text: Tool-First: Designed alongside the compiler and IDE for the best developer experience.
Practical: Focuses on readability and maintainability over clever syntax.

box.title: Kotlin's design focuses on **Pragmatism**. It doesn't try to be a purely functional or purely object-oriented language; instead, it adopts the best features from many paradigms to solve real-world engineering problems efficiently.
Kotlin distinguishes between read-only and mutable variables. Encouraging **immutability** by default makes code more predictable and easier to debug, especially in concurrent environments.

box.code:
val x = 10     // Inferred as Int
val y = "Hello" // Inferred as String

/* Explicit types (when needed) */
var score: Double = 0.0
box.title: Kotlin has a sophisticated type system but doesn't force you to be verbose. Most of the time, the compiler can infer the type of a variable from its initializer.

box.code:
val items = 5
println("You have $items items.")

val price = 10.5
println("Total: ${items * price}")
box.title: Kotlin makes string manipulation intuitive with templates. You can include variable values or even complex expressions directly inside a string using the $ sign.
The when expression is a more powerful and flexible version of the traditional switch. It can be used as a statement or an expression, and its branches can contain complex logic or range checks.

box.code:
var nonNullable: String = "Hello"
// nonNullable = null // COMPILE ERROR

var nullable: String? = "Hello"
nullable = null // OK
box.title: In many languages (like Java), any object reference can be null, leading to the dreaded NullPointerException at runtime. Kotlin solves this by making nullability part of the **Type System**.
To access properties of a nullable type, you must use safe operators. The **Safe Call** returns null instead of crashing, and the **Elvis Operator** provides a default value.
The Kotlin compiler is smart enough to track your null checks. Once you've checked that a variable is not null, the compiler 'smart casts' it to a non-nullable type within that scope.

box.code:
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)

val user1 = User("Alice", 25)
val user2 = user1.copy(age = 26) // Easily create modified copies

println(user1) // User(name=Alice, age=25)
box.title: One of Kotlin's most loved features. By simply adding the data keyword to a class, the compiler automatically generates equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and a copy() function.
Kotlin simplifies class definitions by merging the header and primary constructor. Any initialization logic can be placed in an init block.
Kotlin has no 'static' keyword. Instead, it uses the object keyword to define a **Singleton**—a class that has only one instance, created lazily when first accessed.
Sealed hierarchies allow you to restrict inheritance to a known set of subclasses. This is incredible for representing state and ensures that when expressions covering a sealed type are exhaustive.
Kotlin allows you to extend a class with new functionality without having to inherit from it. This 'Extension' pattern is used throughout the standard library to make APIs more intuitive.
Functions in Kotlin are first-class citizens. You can pass functions as arguments, return them from other functions, and use concise lambda syntax with the it shorthand for single parameters.

box.code:
val numbers = listOf(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)

val doubledEvens = numbers
    .filter { it % 2 == 0 } // [2, 4]
    .map { it * 2 }        // [4, 8]
    .sum()                 // 12
box.title: Kotlin's standard library provides a rich set of functional operators for collections, allowing you to process data pipelines clearly and efficiently.

Coroutines are essentially lightweight threads. You can run hundreds of thousands of coroutines on a single thread without exhausting system resources. They are **non-blocking**, meaning a coroutine can be 'suspended' while waiting for a task, freeing up the thread for other work.
Functions marked with suspend can suspend the execution of a coroutine without blocking the underlying thread. They can only be called from other suspend functions or from a coroutine builder.

list.text: IO: Optimized for network or disk operations.

box.title: Kotlin uses **Structured Concurrency**. Coroutines must be launched within a scope, ensuring that parent tasks track and manage their children. Dispatchers control which thread pool the coroutine runs on.
Flow is an asynchronous stream of values that are computed sequentially. It is similar to a Sequence but for asynchronous values, providing a rich set of operators for transformation and collection.

slides.title: 7. The Modern Ecosystem
slides.subtitle: Beyond the JVM: Android, Cloud, and Multiplatform

box.title: Kotlin is the language of choice for Android. **Jetpack Compose** is Android's modern toolkit for building native UI using a declarative approach, powered entirely by Kotlin.
Ktor is a lightweight, asynchronous framework for building connected applications, while Spring Boot has first-class Kotlin support.
KMP allows you to share core business logic across iOS, Android, Desktop, and Web while keeping the UI layer native to each platform.
Kotlin allows you to create your own Domain Specific Languages (DSLs) using function literals with receivers. This is how libraries like Ktor and Jetpack Compose achieve their clean syntax.
Kotlin provides a way to delegate the getter and setter of a property to another object. Common built-in delegates include lazy (for lazy initialization) and observable.
Kotlin is a pragmatic, versatile language that empowers developers to build safer and more expressive applications across all platforms.
Kotlin works out of the box with JUnit 5. You can use backticks for descriptive test names, making your test results much easier to read.

box.code:
val repository = mockk<UserRepository>()
every { repository.findById(1) } returns User("Alice")

val user = service.getUser(1)
verify { repository.findById(1) }
box.title: While Mockito is common in Java, **MockK** is built specifically for Kotlin. It supports mocking of final classes, extension functions, and co-routines with a clean, functional DSL.
Kotest is a multiplatform testing framework that provides a rich set of assertion styles (like shouldBe) and support for Property-Based Testing.
Kotlin's scope functions execute a block of code within the context of an object. **let** is mostly used for null checks, while **run** is for initialization and computing a result.
**apply** returns the object itself and is used for configuration. **also** is used for additional actions (like logging) that don't modify the object.

Standard collection operations are **eager** (each step creates a new list). **Sequences** allow for **lazy** evaluation, processing elements one by one through the entire pipeline, which is far more efficient for large datasets.

slides.title: 11. The Future: Kotlin 2.0 & Beyond
slides.subtitle: K2 Compiler, Wasm, and Context Receivers

list.text: Cleaner architecture for future language evolution.

box.title: Kotlin 2.0 introduces the **K2 Compiler**, a ground-up rewrite that significantly improves compilation speed and enables more powerful language features by unifying the frontend for all platforms.

box.code: /* Compile Kotlin directly to .wasm binary */
box.title: Kotlin is embracing **WebAssembly** to bring native-like performance to the browser. Kotlin/Wasm allows Jetpack Compose to run directly on the web with incredible speed.
A new way to manage dependencies and context. Instead of passing loggers or database connections as parameters, you can declare that a function requires a specific context to run.
Kotlin is a pragmatic, versatile language that continues to bridge the gap between Java's legacy and the future of multiplatform development.
